words: AXEL ANDERSON: Well, that's quite a story, (chuckles). That would be
interesting too. It was to me.
SAM SCHRAGER: So how did it come about?

speaker: AA
words: Oh I wrote people, they sent me a ticket. So then a farmer by the name

speaker: AA
words: of, their family was Nilson, they come from my home town in Sweden. That

speaker: AA
words: was years ago, you know, they had a farm, they took up homestead there

speaker: AA
words: in Minnesota—Clay County, it's about thirty miles east of Morehead, between

speaker: AA
words: Morehead and Lake Park. They called it Winnipeg Junction them days. There was

speaker: AA
words: a branch line that went up to Manitoba in Canada on the N. P. road,

speaker: AA
words: Northern Pacific. The funny part of it wasI stayed a week in Norway,

speaker: AA
words: Vn Christiana--now they call that town Oslo, that was Christiana then,

speaker: AA
words: that was in 1905. And I stayed a week there, we got on the boat and

speaker: AA
words: we went over to Hull, England, then on the train to Glasgow, Scotland.

speaker: AA
words: And I waited there a week. Then we were on the boat I guess nine or

speaker: AA
words: ten days to get into New York. And got on the train there till we

speaker: AA
words: come to Minneapolis, Minnesota. Course I went around with the kids,

speaker: AA
words: met you know, more kids there, and lots of Swedes,a lot of them when they

speaker: AA
words: got off the train. And we didn't get on the train till about midnight,

speaker: AA
words: again.

speaker: AA
words: And I thought I could stay awake. And I did till--we'd get over to

speaker: AA
words: Winnipeg Junction where I was to get off, they were supposed to meet

speaker: AA
words: me. But five o'clock in the morning, here I was sound asleep you know,

speaker: AA
words: and nobody woke me up. I woke up and it was daylight, you know, it was

speaker: AA
words: all just l.ke it is here. The train was standing still, and I looked up

speaker: AA
words: and I could see Fargo, North Dakota. And here I was, you know,

speaker: AA
words: just a-comin' over, couldn't talk to anybody. So I sat still, I couldn't

speaker: AA
words: get off. So the train started again. Here come the conductor, you know,

speaker: AA
words: and he was looking for a ticket. They'd took mine, see, the night

speaker: AA
words: before, but they didn't wake me up. I was sitting in the chair there

speaker: AA
words: a-sleepin. So he stopped the train and kicked me of£. Got my little

speaker: AA
words: hand bag, and I got off and I thought, "No use to go after the train!"

speaker: AA
words: So I had sense enough to go back, and I walked back to the depot. And

speaker: AA
words: those redcaps was around. There was a nigger, black one, and he tried

speaker: AA
words: everything he could, yotjknow, to talk to me, and of course Icouldn't

speaker: AA
words: understand it. Finally he talked Norwegian—boy, then I was right at

speaker: AA
words: home, I could understand that all right. So he helped me then, you know.

speaker: AA
words: He asked me if I'd had breakfast, and I told him no, I hadn't had

speaker: AA
words: nothing since the day before. He said, "You got any money?" And I

speaker: AA
words: told him yes, I had a little money. So he helped me in there, they

speaker: AA
words: had a beanery at the station, you know. Went in there and got ham and

speaker: AA
words: eggs and potatoes and toast and everything. And he said, "Now you

speaker: AA
words: wait right here," till the train come a little after nine that morning.

speaker: AA
words: Here come a train going east, and he helped me get a ticket on that.

speaker: AA
words: So at eleven o'clock that day, instead of coming from the east at

speaker: AA
words: five o'clock, I come into that station at eleven o'clock from the west.

speaker: AA
words: Nobody there. By god, that was quite a first experience, that's the

speaker: AA
words: first one. That was a pretty good experience right there. Iwas wondering

speaker: AA
words: what the hell was gonna become of me. I was all alone!

speaker: SAM
words: How old were you then?

speaker: AA
words: Well let's see, that was in '05, I must have been nineteen. Yeah, just

speaker: AA
words: about.

speaker: SAM
words: What had you heard about America before you came over that made you want

speaker: SAM
words: to come?

speaker: AA
words: Oh geez, them days, you know, they migrated a lot from all the countries

speaker: AA
words: over here. This country was new, you know. It wasn't like it is now.

speaker: AA
words: Take seventy-five, seventy years back, you know, this country was new.

speaker: AA
words: See in '89 Spokane burnt up, and so you know that's been built since '89.

speaker: AA
words: Well you see Spokane now, so there is one example what the changes has been

speaker: AA
words: here, right here where we're sitting. When I come here in 1907—I put in

speaker: AA
words: two years in Minnesota and Wisconsin, Dakota, knocked around. Had a

speaker: AA
words: good time, bad time, good times, (chuckles)

speaker: SAM
words: What did you do?

speaker: AA
words: Oh I done every damn thing anybody could think of. I was capable of

speaker: AA
words: doing most anything, no matter what I tried I could do it. I used to

speaker: AA
words: drive team, I worked on a farm, I worked with that farmer. I think I

speaker: AA
words: got there the ninth day of June, and I worked there till the plowing was

speaker: AA
words: done in the fall, for the ticket. The ticket cost, I think he paid sixtyfive

speaker: AA
words: dollars to get me over, them days. And I worked June, July, August,

speaker: AA
words: September, October and into November. So we were through plowing and

speaker: AA
words: everything, so there was nothing more to do, only take the chores

speaker: AA
words: around the farm, you know. So I asked him if he was satisfied, I'd go

speaker: AA
words: out and work. He said he was, but he wanted me to stay. That's where

speaker: AA
words: I made my first mistake—I should've stayed and went to school, I could

speaker: AA
words: have went to school then see, that winter, and stayed with him. But

speaker: AA
words: I couldn't see that, I didn't know, I hadn't seen anything on that farm.

speaker: AA
words: So I couldn't tie up on that farm all my life. There I made the first

speaker: AA
words: mistake.

speaker: AA
words: LAURA SCHRAGER: Where'd you go then?

speaker: AA
words: Oh I went out. First I started in on the N. P. Railroad; they were

speaker: AA
words: building a new line there. They had helpers. They raised, they elevated

speaker: AA
words: the track between Hawley and Lake Park, past the farm there, you know.

speaker: AA
words: They put in a fill across that canyon seventy feet higher. That took

speaker: AA
words: off so much grade, you know, they didn't need any helpers any more on

speaker: AA
words: the trains. They could come right straight through from Fargo to St.

speaker: AA
words: Paul without helpers. They had a hill there to go up, so they had to

speaker: AA
words: send a helper with »em always. By god, that took that off. They raised

speaker: AA
words: it up seventy feet, you know, that's quite a bit on the railroad.

speaker: AA
words: And they could distribute that on forty miles, you know, so they almost

speaker: AA
words: come on the level. I worked there awhile, out there on the steam shovel,

speaker: AA
words: worked nights. That's where I met that big Irishman that I brought out

speaker: AA
words: a bottle of whiskey and give him. And he drank all of it, I didn't get

speaker: AA
words: a drop, I wouldn't get a drink of it. He took it all! (chuckles)

speaker: AA
words: Do you remember this guy's name?

speaker: AA
words: No, I don't. No, I couldn't tell you. By god, I don't know. Maybe. . .

speaker: AA
words: Oh, it doesn't matter.

speaker: AA
words: That was in 1905. That's a long time ago. Nope, I can't pick him up.

speaker: AA
words: But you never forgot he drank that whole bottle of whiskey?

speaker: AA
words: No, no, but I didn't buy him any more! And I walked six miles down to

speaker: AA
words: town, and got that and brought it up. I thought I«d be a good fellow you

speaker: AA
words: know, it was cold. That was in November and December, in the fall it's

speaker: AA
words: cold in that country. So when we were going in for dinner, I thought a

speaker: AA
words: good drink before we eat would be just right. He appreciated it. He

speaker: AA
words: said, "Glug-glug-glug-glug-glug-glug" (chugging whiskey) He said,"Lad, that

speaker: AA
words: was a good drink." (chuckles) And he took it all, you could never see it.

speaker: AA
words: It didn't faze him a bit. If that'd been me, I'd've been sleeping under

speaker: AA
words: that steam shovel, (chuckles)

speaker: SAM
words: Where was that farm you worked on and didn't go back to?

speaker: AA
words: It was there at Winnipeg Junction.

speaker: SAM
words: Winnipeg Junction.

speaker: AA
words: This where I worked then at the camp, that was only a mile from the

speaker: AA
words: farm. Part of that railroad come across the farm, that new railroad—

speaker: AA
words: the well the old line did too, down in the bottom of Aravine, the creek.

speaker: AA
words: It made a swing, curve around it, you know, and come on across, hit

speaker: AA
words: the side hill and went up to Lake Park. That took out that swale,

speaker: AA
words: seventy feet, you know, on the rail, that's quite a bit.

speaker: SAM
words: So then, where did you go then after you left there?

speaker: AA
words: Oh after that I went up to Duluth, Superior, Wisconsin. I was up around

speaker: AA
words: Hibbing up in the Iron Belt, and I was out to Eakley, Red River Junction.

speaker: AA
words: I worked for them in the woods first, and then at the mill. A dollar and

speaker: AA
words: eighty cents a day, ten hours, piling lumber. Yeah, I worked in the woods

speaker: AA
words: there, you know, we went out in the woods, we were four of us. We hired

speaker: AA
words: out for thirty-five dollars a month and board. But if you quit, you see,

speaker: AA
words: you get a dollar a day for the day you worked. So then you had in order

speaker: AA
words: to get the thirty-five dollars a month, you had to stay till the job was

speaker: AA
words: done—and I don't know if it clone yet! As long as there was any timber you

speaker: AA
words: know,that company that's working, you never got through. That's the way

speaker: AA
words: they hired ya.

speaker: SAM
words: So when you left you lost that money?

speaker: AA
words: No, no, we got a dollar a day. A dollar and board for each day we worked,

speaker: AA
words: you see. But we didn't get that thirty-five dollars; we got twentysix

speaker: AA
words: (chuckles), twenty-six working days see. Yes, you bet. Then from

speaker: AA
words: there I went to Dakota, oh there I had another experience. That was

speaker: AA
words: pretty good too. We'd been up on the Mesabi Northern there in the Iron

speaker: AA
words: Belt up near Hibbing, Minnesota. There was another kid there, he was

speaker: AA
words: older than me though; he was born at Proctor, out of Duluth, his folks

speaker: AA
words: lived there. And you know, you got into town and he was broke the first

speaker: AA
words: evening. You know, they worked on that railroad, you know, and they

speaker: AA
words: stayed three weeks, two weeks, three weeks, get fifteen, twenty dollars—

speaker: AA
words: into town, blow it, and come back again. I went with 'em one night

speaker: AA
words: and we got around the saloon. Well hell, I didn't get even a room

speaker: AA
words: hardly till I was broke. They kept a-bummin» the hell out of ya around

speaker: AA
words: the saloons. So the next time I come in, and so I got over and got

speaker: AA
words: myself a room and I skidooed—I was out, gone. Next morning, I looked

speaker: AA
words: up some of them. Well, they said they were broke, and "Do you have

speaker: AA
words: any money?" And I said, "No, I ain't got any money." "What happened

speaker: AA
words: to you?" "Oh," I said, " they milked me over and it went." But I had my

speaker: AA
words: little dollars and not very many, but I had a few.

speaker: AA
words: So that day I picked on this feller to go with me, and I went and

speaker: AA
words: looked over the boards. And they sent people out to Leeds, Montana on

speaker: AA
words: the Great Northern to build a reservoir. You see they was building a

speaker: AA
words: reservoir,they had to work on the dump where they built this dam across

speaker: AA
words: the canyon to hold the water, you know, for the locomotives to get water.

speaker: AA
words: We drove that first night and the next day, when we got into Larimer

speaker: AA
words: I think in North Dakota. Going over I could see they were building a dam

speaker: AA
words: there. So I told him—his name was Charlie—and I said, "Let's get off

speaker: AA
words: here, it's the same work we hired out for, same road. Let's get off

speaker: AA
words: and see, we might get on here. The hell with it." That was North

speaker: AA
words: Dakota. So we did. We got off, and we walked back to this camp there, and

speaker: AA
words: they said, "Sure, you bet," there was no trouble, we could get on. So

speaker: AA
words: we got our little packsacks with us and come out to camp that night, and

speaker: AA
words: had supper and got our bed fixed up.

speaker: AA
words: We had to work nights, you know, and they come in there with a trainload

speaker: AA
words: of dump cars, dirt, to make this fill—it was about eighty feet high—

speaker: AA
words: to hold water. And there was frost chunk in there, you know, the frost

speaker: AA
words: go down in that country down about four feet. And they dug that with

speaker: AA
words: them big steam shovels, you know, and there was chunks coming in there

speaker: AA
words: four foot square or more, and they put them in the twenty yard dump cars

speaker: AA
words: on the rail. And they loaded them so that all you had to do was knock

speaker: AA
words: the pin out of them and they dumped. And this was in April, and it was

speaker: AA
words: still cold, frosty. So I tripped one of them cars, and they had it

speaker: AA
words: loaded so only half of it fell out, and the car came back again and

speaker: AA
words: I damn near got caught under it. So I went over to my partner and I

speaker: AA
words: said, "I think we ought to get out of here before I get killed." Oh,"

speaker: AA
words: he said, "kid,"--he was a little older than me—he said, "we'll stay

speaker: AA
words: till we get a few dollars.' "Well," I said, "Okay," and away we went.

speaker: AA
words: By god you know after dinner another train come in, there were thirty,

speaker: AA
words: forty dump cars in the train, and they kept shoving it with the locomotives,

speaker: AA
words: spot them, you see. And we kept a-trippin' em, there were three of us

speaker: AA
words: on there. So we got pretty close together there one time, and five of

speaker: AA
words: them damn cars tipped towards us. It pretty near got all of us. So

speaker: AA
words: then my partner said, "You know," he said, "I think you're right. Let's

speaker: AA
words: get the hell out of here." So we did.

speaker: AA
words: And you know next day we could1 have hired out for the farmers there,

speaker: AA
words: forty dollars a month and board—but only one. It was nice, you know,

speaker: AA
words: in April, the sun was shining, there was a regular breather that day,I

speaker: AA
words: guess. So we thought we'd wait another day. Farmer was a-pullin on

speaker: AA
words: our coattail and wanted us to go out. So we said, "No, we'll wait till

speaker: AA
words: we get to both go to the same farm." Next day come—here was a galldarn

speaker: AA
words: blizzard, and it lasted a week.. There wasn't a farmer in sight no place,

speaker: AA
words: and no work. And then what little money I had, you know, that was gone.

speaker: AA
words: He didn't have a cent when he left Duluth. So I was feeding him, board

speaker: AA
words: and room you know, and we had a little snort here and there. They went—

speaker: AA
words: then so we were clean.

speaker: AA
words: So one morning we took out, we heard about a railroad starting out

speaker: AA
words: from Devils Lake, North Dakota, into Casselton, towards Fargo on the

speaker: AA
words: Great Northern branch line. Went past the Devils Lake; the town is Devils

speaker: AA
words: Lake and it's a lake out there, Devils Lake, too, on the

speaker: AA
words: Indian reservation. So we hiked forty-five miles that day and no

speaker: AA
words: breakfast! And we got over into you know fairly early in the afternoon.

speaker: AA
words: There wereno grass growing under our feet, we were hiking, and fast too.

speaker: AA
words: Had a good early start. The contractors was in the yard there, you know,

speaker: AA
words: getting their tents set up, a little office. Their equipment was coming

speaker: AA
words: in. So we split up so we could cover more ground and maybe get a job.

speaker: AA
words: And I met up with two brothers, Wilson brothers, they were both

speaker: AA
words: Great Northern civil engineers, you know. They were engineers, both of

speaker: AA
words: 'em, but they'd a taken a job. And I guess for that some reason, I

speaker: AA
words: guess what little I could talk I used what I could, I couldn't talk

speaker: AA
words: very much. I told 'em I needed a job pretty bad. So they said, "Kidy

speaker: AA
words: you go up town and stay. And just as soon as we get the horses—we got

speaker: AA
words: the equipment here, but we're waiting for horses to come in—and then you

speaker: AA
words: can get on with us." So I told them I would, but I said, "I can't stay,

speaker: AA
words: I got to have a job." And he said, "Why?" "Well," I said, "I haven't

speaker: AA
words: got a cent, northing, only what I got on my back. I'm broke." And

speaker: AA
words: he said, "I'll take you uptown, and I'll give you a room." Well, " I

speaker: AA
words: said, "still that's fine, but I got a partner here too and he's a better

speaker: AA
words: man than me.' If you could take on both of us, "I said, "okay. We'll

speaker: AA
words: wait, we'll be here." He said, "Can you find him?" And Itold him we

speaker: AA
words: split up so we could cover all of them and see if we couldn't get on.

speaker: AA
words: By god, he said, "Go ahead." I went and found him. He took both of us

speaker: AA
words: in, got us uptown and got us aroom and three meals a day, and we waited

speaker: AA
words: there ten days. And we went out with him, and we stayed till his

speaker: AA
words: job was done. We never went into town, never left the camp from

speaker: AA
words: the day we started till we helped him haul the eqiipment back into

speaker: AA
words: town again and loaded it on the cars. And he never mentioned what he

speaker: AA
words: paid us. We got forty dollars a month and board. So I was lucky.

speaker: SAM
words: How long did that last?

speaker: AA
words: Oh we were there in July. Lasted about three—let's see, in April,

speaker: AA
words: May, June, and into July, that was about a four month job. He had so

speaker: AA
words: many miles, you know, on level ground, it don't take. . .We had this

speaker: AA
words: one dump there, I guess, about thirty-five foot fill across a little

speaker: AA
words: ravine. All horse work—scraper, plow and scrapers. So we drove teams,

speaker: AA
words: worked with the horses. Then we went back to the same old farmer, I

speaker: AA
words: helped him through the harvest. I told him the year before, "I'll be

speaker: AA
words: back." So there in July I was back there, and in August started in

speaker: AA
words: cutting the wheat, and I helped him through the harvest. Went back

speaker: AA
words: up around Duluth again, till next spring I come out here and worked on

speaker: AA
words: the McAldrich. I come out here in April, worked for McAldrich Lumber

speaker: AA
words: Company. You've heard of them? Yeah, McAldrich, I think his boy is,

speaker: AA
words: oh he used to be the president of the Commerce people there in town

speaker: AA
words: a long time here. The old man is dead. He was a nice old man, I met him

speaker: AA
words: several times. He was a nice old man. They logged all that country

speaker: AA
words: around Tensed, you know, where you go over from—that was McAldrich's

speaker: AA
words: outfit. They had railroad all over there in from Tensed and up to the

speaker: AA
words: corner there where you get into Idaho, up over the hill where the

speaker: AA
words: highway goes? He logged that country. I went through there when they

speaker: AA
words: had railroads in there. H*s railroad come out of Tekoa, that's where

speaker: AA
words: he landed logs on the Union Pacific—isn't it?—and brought them into

speaker: AA
words: Spokane. And over the hill there towards Santa was Blackwell. There

speaker: AA
words: were on Santa Creek over throughwhat is that, Emida?

speaker: SAM
words: Yeah. So you worked then around Tensed?

speaker: AA
words: No, no, I never worked there, only just going through. No, I never

speaker: AA
words: worked for McAldrich in the woods. He'd come up and see us at Potlatch

speaker: AA
words: a lot of times. Old man, J. P. McAldrich, he was a nice old man, hell

speaker: AA
words: of a nice fella, just as simple as any of us lumberjacks. I run the

speaker: AA
words: camp out of Clarkia there oh for two years, '39 and '40, '41. We

speaker: AA
words: had 350 men in that camp. That's a big camp. We had twenty-four

speaker: AA
words: skidding cats and an old dozer. I had three assistants; Oscar Hagborn,

speaker: AA
words: the one there, and Clark Lancaster, I guess Clark is dead now, I think,

speaker: AA
words: and Bill Greenwood, he's dead. They were my assistants. We had three

speaker: AA
words: locomotives and twenty-five cats, twenty-four skidding cats and a little

speaker: AA
words: old dozer. That was in "39 and '40.

speaker: SAM
words: What camp was this?

speaker: AA
words: That was Camp 35.

speaker: SAM
words: Was that a car camp?

speaker: AA
words: Yeah. Yes, I set the car camp there. Rutledge had started the grade

speaker: AA
words: in there, which we finished up after they consolidated, after they

speaker: AA
words: emerged, you know, the Potlatch and Rutledge and Clearwater. That's

speaker: AA
words: before the Potlatch Forest started in; there was a Rutledge Timber Company

speaker: AA
words: then,you know. They built that before they got the fire on Marble

speaker: AA
words: Creek, before they put in the Incline, but they never used it. That

speaker: AA
words: timber was left in there. We went in there and they had a spur there

speaker: AA
words: and I put in a Y, you know, so you can turn the locomotives. And

speaker: AA
words: inside of that Y I put that whole damn camp. We had camp cars, bunk

speaker: AA
words: cars, enough there for 350 men to sleep. We had two, three dining

speaker: AA
words: cars and a cook car. (chuckles) And we were sitting in that, rail

speaker: AA
words: all around us, in a pocket, just a little pocket in the hill there.

speaker: AA
words: I'd like to go back there sometime and see it. I think the road

speaker: AA
words: goes in there to Marble yet. I think the forest service used the

speaker: AA
words: if old railroad now for thetruck road.

speaker: SAM
words: Somebody told me that that's the biggest car camp since the Panama

speaker: SAM
words: Canal was built.

speaker: AA
words: (chuckles) I don't know of it. It was quite ajob to get that in.

speaker: AA
words: You only had so much room, you see. And to build that and get it in

speaker: AA
words: there, by god I had to measure them cars, the length of them, and

speaker: AA
words: figure them out, and get them so they'd all fit in. I set the

speaker: AA
words: office car first—that's the first one you set—and then the storeroom,

speaker: AA
words: and then the kitchen car, and then a dining car, and then we put four bunk

speaker: AA
words: cars. And we cut off that track and slid it over and set another one in.

speaker: AA
words: And I put in two bunk cars and a dining car-that dining car set right

speaker: AA
words: across from the kitchen, a big one, long, I think that was seventy foot.

speaker: AA
words: And then filled out with bunk cars again. And then put in a third

speaker: AA
words: track and a fourth track. Then they had a little space in there and I

speaker: AA
words: put in—we had a light plant, you know, and showers and a washroom and

speaker: AA
words: a filing car, which was for the saw filer to stay in. We had a little

speaker: AA
words: corner left and I slid them two cars in, that took up all the space

speaker: AA
words: there was. But that was quite a bit of figuring before I could get it

speaker: AA
words: in. And the rails, we just cut the rails you know and shove it in,

speaker: AA
words: cut her off. And finally we got the main line to go by it and the Y around

speaker: AA
words: it, and the main line on the other side going up.

speaker: SAM
words: One dining car was enough for everybody? You had one for each track?

speaker: AA
words: No, we had two, two big ones. And that cook house run twenty-four hours.

speaker: AA
words: See, the mechanics, they work at night, had to, you know, 'cause we had

speaker: AA
words: all the machines out in the daytime. So they had to work nights to

speaker: AA
words: get 'em ready in the morning. The machine shop, we had that on rails

speaker: AA
words: too—had a lathe and a drill press and a steam hammer and a planter.

speaker: AA
words: And old Ernie Smith was there, he was the machinist. Any damn thing you

speaker: AA

speaker: AA
words: half. You see, they were a crew of twenty-one, I think in that crew. And

words: mile. But then you se—when you set the donkey and set the first road,

words: you took the haul-back around all of it, and come in. See, around the

speaker: AA

speaker: AA
words: the whole works first; and then pull the main line out to the first road.

words: And then after that you see}you could go—like the donkey set here, and you

words: road like this, but the haul-back had to go clean around there see, come here.

words: hundred feet and switched the line over then again, the drop off again

words: like that. They all come in to the same, and keep on that way till

words: and he can't keep up with ya. The same with the team. I was driving

words: know, and hold it until we're moving ahead. It's the feeling you have

words: in your own self. I set the foot down on the handle, you know, just bear

words: can do with it. And they'd set her down too deep, you know, well then

words: It's a feeling you get in yourself. That's something pretty hard to

words: explain, but it's alive--the steam in alive and you're alive. And by

words: god you can feel what you can do with it—you either open up and give it

words: a little more or you ease up a little bit on the throttle. And the

words: same when they run a locomotive, that's the same thing. It's-part of

speaker: AA
words: ya. That jansen bar on that throttle, it's part of you when you sit there

speaker: AA
words: in the cab, you know.

speaker: AA
words: I run the locomotive quite a little bit. You open up, and here yougot

speaker: AA
words: your brake, your air brake, right with you on the train, behind ya. You

speaker: AA
words: can feel it all the way! You sit there and watch and you watch ahead of

speaker: AA
words: you and watch behind you, but it's part of ya. You understand? You

words: own heart pecking. It's part of ya. You feel just what the machine can

words: that's it. If you don't get enough you're stuck and if you get

speaker: AA
words: it was tight, you know, and you break that inch and a half cable. That

speaker: AA

speaker: SAM
words: How did they splice that cable?

speaker: AA
words: They roll them. See they came in strands, you know, and they roll

speaker: AA
words: them. And you unravel two and take the other end and roll in with it,

speaker: AA
words: one off and one on, fall in the same groove. They used to take in a

speaker: AA
words: twenty-four foot splice. See that'd as far as from here to that step.

speaker: AA
words: And that'd be a six strand cable, and you unrolled itr and then you go the

speaker: AA
words: other way again, back and forth till you got enough. Then you had two

speaker: AA
words: spikes, you know, what we call marline spikes, put them through the cable,

speaker: AA
words: open it up, and cut that hemp core out of it inside. It's a hemp core

speaker: AA
words: on all cables,you know, so otherwise they'll wear themself off from the

speaker: AA
words: inside, when the strain is on it going through pulleys and so forth. And

speaker: AA
words: then you sink them two ends, bury them. Or sometimes you knot some and

speaker: AA
words: go under, and go over and under two, three times to tuck the end and

speaker: AA
words: faste n them. But on an old cable the wires are broken, wore so bad,

speaker: AA
words: you know, they break. So you'd go in there and open her up in the middle,

speaker: AA
words: and just roll the ends in and bury it, so your ends was in the middle of

speaker: AA
words: the cable where that little hemp rope was in. And the end of the cable

speaker: AA
words: was stuck in there, you know. Oh, I spliced a lot of it. I seen it done

speaker: AA
words: on the stage, I saw it on a stage there up in Vancouver, B. C. They done

speaker: AA
words: il in four minutes, put in a twenty-four foot splice, two of em, on the

speaker: AA
words: stage. Two inch cable, in fourpiinutes. Had the cable there on the stage,

speaker: AA
words: you know, stretched out, and the two of them, they put in a good looking

speaker: AA
words: splice too, dandy.

speaker: SAM

words: did it take a while to get used tofiow far apart you should be setting

words: them up, would it take time?

speaker: AA
words: No, no, I don't think so, I don't think so. The , you know—once

speaker: AA
words: get that in your, you see it once and then you know it. They had them

speaker: AA
words: on the coast long before we did up there in Idaho. They go out and take

speaker: AA
words: a look and see what they did, and then come up and try to fit it in up in

speaker: AA

speaker: AA
words: o* them on the coast. Take a coast man—I've tried a lot of them hook

speaker: AA

speaker: AA
words: big timber, you see. They had, them days, big timber. Where we had to

speaker: AA
words: set up they were plumb stuck, when we said, "Now, okay, here it is." We

speaker: AA
words: timber on the ground they could put the railroad up on the top of the

speaker: AA
words: hill and log up to it. Where in Idaho the yield is so light, you know,

speaker: AA
words: there's so little to the acre in feet, feetage, so we had to put the

speaker: AA
words: railroad in the creek bottom—you couldn't afford to build a road to the

speaker: AA
words: top there. So we had to come downhill, and then we had to use a different

speaker: AA
words: system and use the high line, you see, instead. We used high lead a little

speaker: AA
words: bit these, but not much. We couldn't get the railroad up high enough to

speaker: AA
words: use it.

speaker: SAM
words: Well, what's the difference between high line and high lead?

speaker: AA
words: The high lead is, you had the spar tree right in front of the donkey,

speaker: AA
words: and you go and anchor your tight line way up on top of the hill there.

speaker: AA
words: They only used to go out a thousand feet, eight hundred to a thousand feet,

speaker: AA
words: and we'd go out from half a mile to a mile. And they had no more,

speaker: AA
words: just that one spar tree after the donkey, that was all, they didn't use

speaker: AA
words: no more. But they had a trolley to go on that one line, you know, they

speaker: AA
words: could pull the log in to the donkey and drop it. Where they used to

speaker: AA
words: get one log, you know, with a thousand feet in it, we put on fifteen,

speaker: AA

speaker: AA
words: Such small timber,you know, in scale, see, to the big ones.

speaker: AA

speaker: AA
words: a bit of experience and know hoO when you go out there and have

speaker: AA
words: a nile and you put up eight or ten spar trees or spar poles. On the

speaker: AA

words: up and raised it, and stuck it up. 'Cause we had to put up so many, we

words: so they're—you know, you go over three or four ravines and figure how

speaker: AA
words: high that pole is gonna be where you get a little pressure on it. . .

words: of it, 'cause we had to have a side pull on her too, you see, to hold it.

words: That took quite a bit of ingenuity and know-how to do that. So a

speaker: AA
words: coast man come in and he was stuck, that was way beyond him. He never seen

words: was a foreman the first thing! He knew more than the whole damn crews

speaker: AA
words: put tog|her. One of our men that come up from Idaho out to the coast,

speaker: AA
words: 'course you could stick him on anything, any part of it. Yeah, I seen

speaker: AA
words: then) logging on the coast there, but you they get a trainload

speaker: AA
words: sometime, you know, they get one log and that's a carload! And sometime

speaker: AA
